message.content:
Adversarially verify this finding about a design doc (the doc is at /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt; the repo is /home/jleechan/projects/worktree_runner; you may use WebSearch/WebFetch and read files). Try to REFUTE it. Finding from the "infra-fit" reviewer, severity critical:
TITLE: gha-isolated design has zero disk-management story — the single largest incident class in the existing fleet
DETAIL: The proposal's generated config (src/config.rs in /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt) sets only memory/cpus (4G/2 CPU) — no VM disk size, no disk-pressure threshold, no cleanup hooks. The existing repo's history shows disk exhaustion is THE recurring incident: /home/jleechan/projects/worktree_runner/self-hosted-colima/README.md documents a 60 GiB VM that 'filled up under normal use' and the 120 GiB sizing table (~50 GB overlay diffs, ~20 GB workdirs, ~30 GB headroom); the fleet needed three layers of mitigation — a per-job pre-job hook that clears pip cache + workspace below 5 GB free (self-hosted-colima/scripts/pre-job-hook.sh and self-hosted-oss/pre-job-hook.sh, DISK_MIN_KB=5242880), a daily cron (scripts/cleanup.sh: git gc, diag prune, docker prune), and a weekly hard-cleanup (scripts/hard-cleanup.sh: down → prune -af → up, auto-skip below 70%, bails if a runner is mid-job). The 2026-06-14 disk-fill incident (memory: limactl-shell cron silent no-op, PR #7582) came from exactly this class. Note: true per-job ephemeral VMs (the proposal's Tart/libvirt path) would eliminate most of this class by giving each job a fresh disk image — but only if the design actually says so and sizes the base image; the skeleton does neither.
RECOMMENDATION: Any adoption must carry over: explicit VM/volume disk sizing (120 GiB rationale), the ACTIONS_RUNNER_HOOK_JOB_STARTED pre-job disk-cleanup hook (5 GB threshold + chmod -R u+w on _actions for the read-only-tar bug), and scheduled cleanup for any persistent state. If ephemeral VMs are used, document that base-image freshness replaces the cleanup crons and size the image ≥ per-job peak (checkout + toolchains + artifacts).
Is the finding factually correct and does the recommendation make sense? If the factual basis is wrong or the severity is inflated, refute it. Default to refuted=true only if you have concrete contrary evidence or the claim is unsupported after checking.

input.reasoning:
Every factual claim in the finding was verified against the design doc and the repo, and I found no basis to refute it.

(1) Design doc has zero disk story — CONFIRMED. I read all 292 lines of /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt. The generated config (src/config.rs, lines 212-251) sets only `memory: 4G` / `cpus: 2` (plus `pids_limit: 100` on the Docker path). There is no VM disk size, no base-image sizing, no disk-pressure threshold, no cleanup hook, no ACTIONS_RUNNER_HOOK_JOB_STARTED, and no scheduled cleanup anywhere in the doc. Line 10 says "Use GitHub Scale Sets + ephemeral runners by default" but never states that ephemeral VMs replace cleanup, nor sizes the image — exactly as the finding says ("the skeleton does neither").

(2) Disk exhaustion is the documented recurring incident class — CONFIRMED. /home/jleechan/projects/worktree_runner/self-hosted-colima/README.md line 200: "The previous default of 60 GiB filled up under normal use"; lines 186-198 contain the "Why 120 GiB" table with ~50 GB overlay diffs (line 193), ~20 GB workdirs (line 194), ~30+ GB headroom (line 197); line 173: workdir artifacts are "enough to fill the 120 GB VM in a few hours of active CI."

(3) Three mitigation layers exist exactly as described: (a) pre-job hooks — both /home/jleechan/projects/worktree_runner/self-hosted-colima/scripts/pre-job-hook.sh (line 12) and /home/jleechan/projects/worktree_runner/self-hosted-oss/pre-job-hook.sh (line 26) have `DISK_MIN_KB="${DISK_MIN_KB:-5242880}"` (5 GB) plus `chmod -R u+w "${RUNNER_WORKDIR}/_actions"` for the read-only-tar bug; (b) daily cleanup — cleanup-inner.sh does `git gc --prune=now --aggressive` on all workdirs, prunes _diag/ files >1 day, and runs `docker system prune`; (c) weekly hard-cleanup — hard-cleanup.sh has SKIP_THRESHOLD_PCT=70 auto-skip (line 45/67), bails if any runner has an in-flight job (UUID-subdir check, lines 72-86), and does compose down → `docker system prune -af` → up (lines 100-115).

(4) The 2026-06-14 disk-fill incident and PR #7582 are documented in the user's persistent memory (feedback_2026-06-15_limactl_shell_cron_silent_noop: "Root cause of 2026-06-14 disk-fill incident; fixed in PR #7582").

Severity assessment: "critical" is defensible, not inflated. The proposal would replace a fleet whose single most documented, most-mitigated failure mode is disk exhaustion (three independent mitigation layers plus a real incident), and the replacement design carries over none of it. The one counterargument — that ephemeral per-job VMs would inherently solve it — is preemptively and correctly handled by the finding itself: the doc neither commits to per-job-fresh disk images nor sizes them, and "ephemeral runner" (GitHub's one-job-per-registration semantics) does not by itself imply a fresh disk. The recommendation (carry over 120 GiB sizing rationale, the 5 GB pre-job hook with the _actions chmod, scheduled cleanup for persistent state, or explicitly document that base-image freshness replaces crons and size the image for per-job peak) is technically sound and matches the repo's own hard-won practices.
